mnemonics.title: D-S-I-G-N Properties
mnemonics.content: Digital Signatures provide: D-A-I-N → Digital (cryptographic), Authentication (who sent), Integrity (not tampered), Non-repudiation (can't deny), Greed-proof (can't double-spend) - wait, no... just remember 'D-A-I-N' - Digital proves Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation.

mnemonics.title: ECDSA Steps
mnemonics.content: SIGN: S-elect random k, G-enerate point (kG), a-lculate r = x-coordinate mod n, c-alculate s = k^(-1)(hash + d*r) mod n, N-once never reuse.

mnemonics.title: Verify ECDSA
mnemonics.content: VERIFY: V-erify s is not zero, u1 = hash * s^(-1) mod n, u2 = r * s^(-1) mod n, P = u1*G + u2*Q, Check if r ≡ x-coordinate(P) mod n

acronyms.usage: The signature scheme used by Bitcoin and Ethereum
US Federal standard, based on discrete logarithm problem
Framework for managing public keys and digital certificates
Symmetric alternative to digital signatures, uses shared secret key

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two points on an elliptical curve. Draw a line through them - it intersects the curve at a third point. Reflect that point across the x-axis to get the sum. This geometric operation becomes algebraic (modular arithmetic) in the finite field.

Left side: Sender hashes message → encrypts hash with private key → attaches signature. Right side: Receiver hashes message → decrypts signature with sender's public key → compares both hashes. Match = valid.

Think of the private key as a secret number 'd'. The public key is 'Q = d × G' where G is a fixed generator point. You can compute Q from d easily (one multiplication), but computing d from Q requires solving the discrete logarithm - like finding which multiplication step produced the result.

analogies.analogy: A physical signature can be copied or forged. A digital signature is like sealing a letter with wax - any tampering breaks the seal, and only the sender has the unique seal stamp (private key).

The private key is like your fingerprint - unique to you, never shared. The public key is like your name that you tell everyone. Anyone can say 'Hello to [Your Name]' but only your fingerprint can do things only you can do.

The hash function is like a document fingerprint. Instead of signing a 10MB file (computationally expensive), we sign just its unique 32-byte fingerprint. Any change to the file produces a completely different fingerprint, invalidating the signature.
